title: Inventory Management Discovery Call – Meera Kapoor (CEO)
transcript:
BA: Good morning, Meera. Thanks for making the time. Today I'd like to understand your biggest challenges around inventory management across your 120 stores. Let's start with what keeps you up at night.
CEO: Morning! Honestly, what keeps me up at night is dead stock. We're a fashion jewellery brand — trends move in weeks, not months. And right now, we have no intelligent way to move inventory from stores where it's not selling to stores where it could sell. Last financial year, dead stock and forced markdowns cost us around ₹8 crore. That's real money walking out the door.
BA: ₹8 crore is significant. Is that across both imitation and real jewellery lines?
CEO: Almost entirely imitation. Real jewellery holds intrinsic value — you can rework gold pieces. But imitation? Once a trend dies, those pieces are worthless. And imitation is 70 percent of our volume. So it's the bulk of the problem.
BA: What makes redistribution so difficult today?
CEO: Everything is manual and slow. If the Lucknow store has pieces that aren't moving, by the time the manager flags it, the regional coordinator arranges a transfer, and it routes through our Delhi warehouse — we've lost three weeks. In our business, three weeks is a death sentence for a product. The piece arrives at the new store already half-dead.
BA: So the transfer process itself is a bottleneck. What about visibility — do you know in real time what's sitting where?
CEO: Not really. We have an ERP, it's about eight years old. It technically tracks inventory. But the data is garbage. Store staff enter things late, or wrong, or not at all. Last quarter's physical count found ₹2.3 crore in discrepancies — stock that was misplaced, miscategorized, or just unaccounted for. So nobody trusts the system. Regional managers pick up the phone and call stores directly. It's become a workaround culture.
BA: So inaccurate data and lack of real-time visibility. Would you say the data quality issue is the root cause of most of the other problems?
CEO: I wouldn't go that far. The data is bad, yes, but even if we had perfect data, we still wouldn't know what to do with it. We don't have any analytics layer. Our POS captures every sale, so the raw data exists, but nobody is looking at it proactively to spot trends or trigger stock movements. It just sits there. The problem is more about intelligence than data, in my opinion.
BA: Interesting. So if I asked you to prioritize — fix the data foundation, or build the analytics and redistribution intelligence — which comes first?
CEO: The intelligence layer. Absolutely. Give me a system that can look at sales velocity across stores and say, "Move these 200 choker sets from Lucknow to Pune," and I'll save crores overnight. The data issue is annoying but it's not the bottleneck.
BA: I want to push back gently on that. If the underlying inventory data is unreliable — ₹2.3 crore in discrepancies as you said — how would an intelligence layer make accurate redistribution recommendations?
CEO: …Fair point. I suppose you'd need reasonably clean data to feed the algorithms. Okay, maybe I'm getting ahead of myself. But I don't want to spend two years fixing data before we see any value. It has to be quick.
BA: Understood. Let me ask about seasonal dynamics. How do peak periods like Diwali and wedding season affect things?
CEO: It's the mirror image of the dead stock problem. During peak season — October to February — we lose sales because our best-performing stores run out of bestsellers. Last Diwali, we estimated about ₹4 crore in lost revenue. Customers came in asking for pieces they'd seen on social media, and we simply didn't have them. Meanwhile, those exact items were sitting in stores in the Northeast where demand was low. It's heartbreaking.
BA: So dead stock off-peak and stockouts during peak — same root cause, different symptoms?
CEO: Exactly. Two sides of the same coin.
BA: What about demand forecasting? How do you currently plan what to produce and where to send it?
CEO: We plan seasonally — four main collections a year. Our head of design has been doing this for 25 years and has great instincts. Our hit rate is pretty good, honestly — 60 to 65 percent of new styles perform well. The issue isn't what we make, it's where we send it. Allocation is where we fail.
BA: A 35 to 40 percent miss rate means roughly a third of your new inventory is at risk of becoming dead stock from day one. Doesn't that suggest product-market fit is also part of the problem, not just allocation?
CEO: Well, no, I wouldn't characterize it that way. Even those pieces that don't sell in one region often do well somewhere else. The Jaipur example I always cite — a collection that got 20 percent sell-through in Delhi hit 78 percent in Jaipur when someone had the sense to move it. So it's not that the products are wrong, they're just in the wrong places.
BA: That's a compelling example. Let me shift to users. Who would use this system day-to-day?
CEO: The six regional coordinators — they'd be the power users. My supply chain head, Rajesh, would want dashboards and the big picture view. And the 120 store managers would need something very simple. If it's more than three taps on a screen, they won't use it. Our store staff aren't particularly tech-savvy. But I should say — the store managers have incredible local knowledge. Mrs. Sharma in Kolkata knows her customer base inside out. Any system we build has to respect that ground-level intelligence, not override it.
BA: So the system needs to blend data-driven recommendations with human local expertise?
CEO: Exactly. Collaborative, not dictatorial.
BA: One last question. Have you considered direct store-to-store transfers, bypassing the warehouse?
CEO: We've discussed it. It would solve the speed problem overnight. But for real jewellery — we're talking pieces worth ₹2 to ₹5 lakh each — I can't have those moving between stores without bulletproof tracking. We had a pilferage incident two years ago, ₹12 lakh. It took three months to investigate. So until we have airtight traceability, I'm not comfortable with direct transfers for the high-value lines. Imitation, maybe — the risk is lower.
BA: That's very helpful. I think we've covered significant ground. I'll put together a summary and schedule a follow-up with Rajesh for the operational deep-dive. Thank you, Meera.
CEO: Thank you! Looking forward to seeing how this shapes up.
